analysis.summary: The contract implements basic Reg D 506(c) controls but has a critical deficiency in accredited investor verification—it relies solely on issuer attestation without any on-chain proof reference or third-party verification mechanism. Transfer restrictions and lockup enforcement are present but investor count tracking has edge cases that could be exploited.

results.rule: 1. Investor cap enforcement (max 2,000 or fewer investors for certain exemptions)
results.finding: MAX_INVESTORS is set to 1500 and enforced in mint(), but investorCount never decrements when investors transfer out their full balance, potentially blocking legitimate new investors while actual investor count is lower.

results.rule: 2. Accredited investor verification — must require proof, not just self-certification
results.finding: setAccredited() allows owner to flag any address as accredited without any on-chain proof reference, hash, or third-party verifier integration. Contract comments explicitly state 'Issuer self-attests accreditation. No on-chain proof reference stored.' This fails the 506(c) requirement for reasonable steps to verify accredited status.

results.rule: 3. Transfer restrictions — resale prohibited without registration or another exemption
results.finding: The _update() override enforces that all secondary transfers require the recipient to be marked as accredited, effectively restricting transfers to verified/whitelisted addresses only.

results.rule: 4. No general solicitation beyond verified accredited investors
results.finding: General solicitation is a marketing/distribution concern that occurs off-chain and cannot be enforced by smart contract logic. This rule is outside the scope of on-chain enforcement.
TRANSFER_LOCKUP of 180 days is enforced in _update() by checking block.timestamp against mintTimestamp[from]. Secondary transfers are blocked until lockup expires.

No jurisdiction tracking, country codes, or geographic restriction logic exists in the contract. There is no mechanism to enforce US-only participation or block restricted jurisdictions as may be required for Reg D compliance.

criticalIssues: Accredited investor verification lacks any on-chain proof mechanism—506(c) requires issuers to take 'reasonable steps' to verify accredited status, not merely self-attest
No jurisdiction controls to ensure only US persons participate or to block restricted jurisdictions
investorCount tracking is one-way (only increments) and could prevent legitimate minting when actual unique holders fall below cap
recommendations: Integrate a third-party accreditation verifier contract or store proof hashes (e.g., keccak256 of verification document/attestation ID) when setting accredited status
Add jurisdiction mapping (address => bytes2 countryCode) and enforce US residency or block sanctioned jurisdictions in mint() and _update()
Implement investorCount decrement logic in _update() when a sender's balance becomes zero after transfer
Consider adding a verificationTimestamp mapping to ensure accreditation checks are current (506(c) verification has temporal relevance)
Add event emissions for compliance audit trail: AccreditationSet, JurisdictionSet events with relevant parameters
